summary: The project has resolved 81 of 118 tracked issues (69%), with all 37 open items concentrated entirely in three problems: q4 (22), q5 (14), and q6 (1). Most problem lists are fully resolved, but the core first_proof_1 question set shows low solvability scores (median ~16%), indicating that headline closures may rest on shaky or incomplete proofs that warrant verification before declaring victory.
problems: q4 is the primary blocker with 22 open issues despite 23 resolved — a roughly 50% unresolved rate suggesting a persistent structural obstacle in the proof.
q5 has 14 open vs 10 resolved (58% open), the worst open-ratio in the project and a clear bottleneck.
Solvability scores are alarmingly low across nearly all questions (q3 13%, q5 14%, q4 16%, q9 16%, q8 9%), implying current approaches have low expected success even where issues appear closed.
Mismatch between 'resolved' status and low Opus solvability suggests some closures are premature or insufficiently verified.
Zero in-progress items indicates a lack of active work momentum — the team is not currently engaging the open backlog.
highlights: High overall resolution rate (81 resolved, 69%) with 17 of 20 problem lists completely cleared.
q7 (58%) and q10 (57%) have strong solvability scores, marking them as the most tractable and reliable results.
All AIM problem lists, Erdos problems, and first_proof_2 are fully resolved, showing broad coverage.
Very low daily spend ($0.19, 2 runs) leaves ample budget headroom for an intensive verification or solving push.
Consistent use of a single strong model (claude_opus_5-4) across best proofs enables clean comparative evaluation.

suggested_todos.title: Concentrate effort on q4's 22 open issues to identify the shared structural obstacle blocking ~half its proofs
Audit q5's 14 open issues and reassess whether the current proof strategy is viable given its 14% solvability
Re-verify 'resolved' issues on q4 and q5 against the low solvability scores to catch premature closures
Close out the single remaining open issue on q6 for a quick complete-problem win
Document the successful strategies from high-solvability q7 and q10 and attempt to transfer them to stuck problems
Schedule and budget a dedicated solving run for the 37 open issues given low current usage

mistakes: Marking issues 'resolved' while underlying solvability remains low (q3 13%, q8 9%) — closures may not reflect genuinely verified proofs.
Repeatedly accumulating open issues on q4 and q5 without a strategy pivot, suggesting persistence with an approach that is not converging.
No issues placed in-progress, indicating the team starts/closes work without tracked iteration, hampering diagnosis of failed approaches.
Relying on a single model for all best proofs without cross-model validation, risking systematic blind spots in verification.
Likely misjudging problem difficulty — effort distribution does not align with solvability, leaving low-score problems under-analyzed before being declared done.
Potential misread of hypotheses on the hardest questions (q8, q5), inferred from high open-issue counts paired with low solvability.
